content.problemStatement:
Treasure Hunting is a great computer game that has attracted generations of Bytelandian children.
In the game, there is a maze divided into N×NN \times NN×N squares.


Dave starts in the top-left corner, which is square (1,1)(1,1)(1,1), and needs to go to the bottom-right corner, which is square (N,N)(N,N)(N,N).
Some of the squares are blocked and some of the squares contain treasures.
Dave needs to capture all the treasures in the maze before going to square (N,N)(N,N)(N,N).
In each second, Dave can go to one of its four adjacent squares (if the destination is not blocked).
Find the earliest time that Dave can reach the destination (N,N)(N,N)(N,N) after collecting all the treasures in the maze.


Input Format

The first line contains TTT, the number of test cases. Then TTT test cases follow.
The first line of each testcase contains NNN, the size of the maze
The NNN following lines describe the maze. The meaning of the symbols is as follows:

'.' : an empty square
'*' : a treasure
'#' : a blocked square




Output Format
For each test case, print in a single line the earliest time that Dave can reach the destination after collecting all the treasures. If Dave cannot reach the destination, print -1.
constraints: The number of treasures in the maze does not exceed 13.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. The first line of each test case contains N, the size of the maze. The N following lines describe the maze. The meaning of the symbols is as follows: '.' : an empty square, '*' : a treasure, '#' : a blocked square
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print in a single line the earliest time that Dave can reach the destination after collecting all the treasures. If Dave cannot reach the destination, print -1.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a breadth-first search (BFS) algorithm with bit manipulation to keep track of the collected treasures. The BFS queue will store the current position, the collected treasures, and the time taken so far. The algorithm will explore all possible directions from the current position and update the collected treasures and time accordingly.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2 * 2^T) per test case, where N is the size of the maze and T is the number of treasures.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2 * 2^T) for the BFS queue and the visited array.

code.Python3:
from collections import deque

DX = [0, 0, 1, -1]
DY = [1, -1, 0, 0]

def solve_maze(n, maze):
    total_treasures = 0
    treasure_positions = []
    
    # Identify treasures and map them to bits
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(n):
            if maze[i][j] == '*':
                treasure_positions.append((i, j))
                total_treasures += 1
    
    all_treasures_mask = (1 << total_treasures) - 1
    
    # BFS Queue
    q = deque([(0, 0, 0, 0)])  # (x, y, collected_bitmask, time)
    visited = [[[False for _ in range(1 << total_treasures)] for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(n)]
    visited[0][0][0] = True
    
    while q:
        x, y, collected, time = q.popleft()
        
        # Collect a treasure if at the position
        for t, (tx, ty) in enumerate(treasure_positions):
            if maze[x][y] == '*' and x == tx and y == ty:
                collected |= (1 << t)
        
        # Check if all treasures collected and reached end
        if collected == all_treasures_mask and x == n - 1 and y == n - 1:
            return time
        
        # Explore all four directions
        for dir in range(4):
            nx, ny = x + DX[dir], y + DY[dir]
            
            if 0 <= nx < n and 0 <= ny < n and maze[nx][ny] != '#' and not visited[nx][ny][collected]:
                visited[nx][ny][collected] = True
                q.append((nx, ny, collected, time + 1))
    
    return -1  # If destination is not reachable

for _ in range(int(input())):
    n = int(input())
    maze = [input() for _ in range(n)]
    print(solve_maze(n, maze))
